article_meta.title: | Updated November 25, 2025 9:00am EST Nearly 1 in 4 American households

reasoning: The model returned an empty array for an article that contains some defensible author-level bias instances, though the article is relatively data-driven and heavily relies on quoted sources. The article does contain some author-prose bias worth flagging: the embedded headline 'NEW CAR PRICES SOAR TO HISTORIC HIGH AS BUYERS FACE MOUNTING STICKER SHOCK' (repeated twice) uses sensationalist language in the author's editorial framing. The phrase 'Inflation has continued to strain the budgets of American households' is an author-prose opinion/framing statement presented as fact. The article also arguably has slant issues - it frames the economic situation primarily through a negative lens despite noting slowdowns in growth rates. However, this is a borderline case since the article is largely data-driven reporting with extensive sourcing, so returning [] is not as egregious as in the calibration Example 3.

custom_reasoning.attribution_rule_compliance: The model returned an empty array, so there are zero attribution rule violations. No quoted text was flagged.
custom_reasoning.false_positive_rate: No items were flagged at all, so there are zero false positives.
custom_reasoning.false_negative_rate: The model missed some defensible author-level bias: (1) 'Inflation has continued to strain the budgets of American households' - author presents opinion as fact; (2) the repeated sensationalist cross-promotion headline 'NEW CAR PRICES SOAR TO HISTORIC HIGH AS BUYERS FACE MOUNTING STICKER SHOCK'; (3) 'MIDDLE-INCOME AMERICANS PESSIMISTIC ABOUT THEIR FINANCIAL FUTURE AMID PERSISTENT INFLATION' - sensationalist headline insertion; (4) possible Negativity Bias/Slant in overall framing. These are moderate instances though, as the article is largely data-driven reporting.
custom_reasoning.bias_type_accuracy: No bias types were applied, so no incorrect labels. N/A essentially.
